question: Customers who ordered only in the last 6 months
explanation: MIN(order_date) >= 6 months ago means every single order this customer placed falls within the recent window — no older history exists.
query: SELECT customer_id FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING MIN(order_date) >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '6 months';

question: Total orders per day including days with zero orders
explanation: generate_series fills every date in the range. LEFT JOIN pads missing days. COUNT returns 0 for days with no orders.
query: WITH dates AS ( SELECT generate_series(MIN(order_date), MAX(order_date), INTERVAL '1 day') AS day FROM orders ) SELECT d.day, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count FROM dates d LEFT JOIN orders o ON d.day = o.order_date GROUP BY d.day ORDER BY d.day;

question: Department with the most employees
explanation: COUNT per department, ORDER BY descending, LIMIT 1 returns the single department with the most headcount.
query: SELECT department_id, COUNT(*) AS employee_count FROM employees GROUP BY department_id ORDER BY employee_count DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Find gaps in employee IDs
explanation: ROW_NUMBER produces a sequential counter. Wherever the real id differs from rn, a gap has been found in the sequence.
query: WITH numbered AS ( SELECT id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY id) AS rn FROM employees ) SELECT rn + 1 AS missing_id FROM numbered WHERE id <> rn;

question: Employees hired before their managers
explanation: Self-join compares hire dates. Employees hired before their own manager are veterans who predate their boss.
query: SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager, e.hire_date, m.hire_date AS mgr_hire_date FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id WHERE e.hire_date < m.hire_date;

question: Departments with average salary above the overall company average
explanation: HAVING compares each department's average against a scalar subquery of the company-wide average.
query: SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department_id HAVING AVG(salary) > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

question: Employee with the highest number of dependents
explanation: COUNT per employee in the dependents table. ORDER BY descending LIMIT 1 finds the employee with the most.
query: SELECT employee_id, COUNT(*) AS dependent_count FROM dependents GROUP BY employee_id ORDER BY dependent_count DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Customer with the longest gap between consecutive orders
explanation: LAG gives the prior order date per customer. Date difference = gap. MAX per customer then LIMIT 1 finds the biggest gap overall.
query: WITH ordered AS ( SELECT customer_id, order_date, LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev FROM orders ), gaps AS ( SELECT customer_id, order_date - prev AS gap_days FROM ordered WHERE prev IS NOT NULL ) SELECT customer_id, MAX(gap_days) AS longest_gap FROM gaps GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY longest_gap DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Customers who ordered all products in a specific category
explanation: Filter sales to the category's products. Compare each customer's distinct product count to the total in that category.
query: SELECT customer_id FROM sales WHERE product_id IN (SELECT product_id FROM products WHERE category_id = 1) GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products WHERE category_id = 1);

question: Most recent order date per customer
explanation: Simple MAX aggregate over order_date returns the latest order date for each customer.
query: SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) AS last_order_date FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id;

question: List all employees with their manager names
explanation: LEFT JOIN so the root employee (CEO) also appears in results — with NULL as their manager name.
query: SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

question: Employees with the same salary as their manager
explanation: Self-join on manager_id. Equality on salary reveals cases where employee and manager earn identically.
query: SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager, e.salary FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id WHERE e.salary = m.salary;

question: Products with total sales above the average sales amount
explanation: CTE computes the overall average. HAVING compares each product's total revenue against that benchmark.
query: WITH avg_sales AS (SELECT AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM sales) SELECT product_id, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales GROUP BY product_id HAVING SUM(amount) > (SELECT avg_amount FROM avg_sales);

question: Number of employees hired each year
explanation: EXTRACT YEAR groups employees by their hire year. COUNT gives the annual hiring count.
query: SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM hire_date) AS hire_year, COUNT(*) AS count FROM employees GROUP BY hire_year ORDER BY hire_year;

question: Employee count per job title within each department
explanation: Two-level GROUP BY produces counts at the intersection of department and job title.
query: SELECT department_id, job_title, COUNT(*) AS employee_count FROM employees GROUP BY department_id, job_title;

question: Find root employees — those with no manager assigned
explanation: manager_id IS NULL marks the top of the hierarchy — the CEO or any root-level employee.
query: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL;

question: Average salary by department and job title
explanation: GROUP BY on two dimensions gives granular salary insights at the department × role level.
query: SELECT department_id, job_title, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department_id, job_title;

question: Median salary of all employees
explanation: PostgreSQL's PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) computes the exact interpolated median over all employee salaries.
query: SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS median_salary FROM employees;

question: Employees promoted more than once
explanation: Counting rows in the promotions table per employee. Greater than 1 means at least two promotions have occurred.
query: SELECT employee_id, COUNT(*) AS promotion_count FROM promotions GROUP BY employee_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

question: Total sales by product category
explanation: JOIN brings in the category from products. GROUP BY and SUM aggregate revenue per category.
query: SELECT p.category_id, SUM(s.amount) AS total_sales FROM sales s JOIN products p ON s.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY p.category_id;

question: Top 3 products by total sales amount
explanation: Aggregate per product, sort descending, LIMIT 3 returns the top three best-selling products.
query: SELECT product_id, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales GROUP BY product_id ORDER BY total_sales DESC LIMIT 3;

question: Employees who joined after their department was created
explanation: JOIN on department_id then compare hire_date vs creation_date — a standard new-hire vs founding-member check.
query: SELECT e.* FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id WHERE e.hire_date > d.creation_date;

question: Customers with no sales records
explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps all customers. A NULL sale_id confirms that customer never made any purchase.
query: SELECT c.* FROM customers c LEFT JOIN sales s ON c.customer_id = s.customer_id WHERE s.sale_id IS NULL;

question: Second highest salary company-wide
explanation: Inner subquery finds the global maximum. The outer MAX finds the highest value strictly below it.
query: SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_highest FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

question: Products with sales only in the current month
explanation: Both earliest and latest sale dates must fall within the current month — meaning no older sales exist for the product.
query: SELECT product_id FROM sales GROUP BY product_id HAVING MAX(sale_date) >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE) AND MIN(sale_date) >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE);

question: Employees with consecutive workdays
explanation: Date minus row_number groups consecutive dates under the same grp value, enabling a COUNT per streak.
query: WITH attendance AS ( SELECT employee_id, work_date, work_date - ROW_NUMBER() OVER ( PARTITION BY employee_id ORDER BY work_date ) * INTERVAL '1 day' AS grp FROM work_log ) SELECT employee_id, COUNT(*) AS consecutive_days FROM attendance GROUP BY employee_id, grp HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

question: Average number of orders per customer
explanation: Inner query counts orders per customer. Outer AVG averages those per-customer counts.
query: SELECT AVG(order_count) AS avg_orders_per_customer FROM ( SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id ) sub;

question: Employees who worked on more than 5 projects
explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT) prevents duplicate project counting. HAVING > 5 identifies the most project-active employees.
query: SELECT employee_id, COUNT(DISTINCT project_id) AS project_count FROM project_assignments GROUP BY employee_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT project_id) > 5;

question: Total products sold each day
explanation: SUM of quantity per day shows total units moved — useful for tracking daily sales volume trends.
query: SELECT sale_date, SUM(quantity) AS total_qty_sold FROM sales GROUP BY sale_date ORDER BY sale_date;

question: Customers with orders totaling more than $10,000
explanation: SUM per customer. HAVING > 10000 filters only the high-value customers.
query: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_amount FROM sales GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(amount) > 10000;

question: Employees who have never received a bonus
explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps all employees. A NULL bonus_id confirms no bonus record exists for that employee.
query: SELECT e.* FROM employees e LEFT JOIN bonuses b ON e.id = b.employee_id WHERE b.bonus_id IS NULL;

question: Department with the lowest average salary
explanation: ORDER BY avg_salary ascending (the default) then LIMIT 1 picks the lowest-paid department.
query: SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department_id ORDER BY avg_salary LIMIT 1;

question: Cumulative order count per customer over time
explanation: COUNT(*) OVER with ORDER BY accumulates a running count of orders per customer chronologically.
query: SELECT customer_id, order_date, COUNT(*) OVER ( PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date ) AS cumulative_orders FROM orders;

question: Customers who ordered from only one category
explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT category_id) = 1 means all purchases belong to a single product category.
query: SELECT customer_id FROM sales s JOIN products p ON s.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.category_id) = 1;

question: Employees with manager names including those without managers
explanation: LEFT JOIN includes the root employee (CEO). Their manager_name column appears as NULL.
query: SELECT e.name AS employee_name, m.name AS manager_name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

question: Products with sales increasing every month for the last 3 months
explanation: Two LAG values give the prior two months. All three must be strictly increasing for the product to qualify.
query: WITH monthly_sales AS ( SELECT product_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', sale_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales WHERE sale_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '3 months' GROUP BY product_id, month ), ranked AS ( SELECT product_id, month, total_sales, LAG(total_sales,1) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY month) AS prev1, LAG(total_sales,2) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY month) AS prev2 FROM monthly_sales ) SELECT DISTINCT product_id FROM ranked WHERE total_sales > prev1 AND prev1 > prev2;

question: Recursive query — all descendants of a given manager
explanation: Anchor = the given manager (id = 1). Each recursion finds their direct and indirect reports at any depth.
query: WITH RECURSIVE subordinates AS ( SELECT id, name, manager_id FROM employees WHERE id = 1 UNION ALL SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id FROM employees e JOIN subordinates s ON e.manager_id = s.id ) SELECT * FROM subordinates WHERE id != 1;

question: Department with the highest salary variance
explanation: VAR_SAMP computes sample variance (Bessel's correction). High variance = wide salary spread within the department.
query: SELECT department_id, VAR_SAMP(salary) AS salary_variance FROM employees GROUP BY department_id ORDER BY salary_variance DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Difference between consecutive orders per customer
explanation: LAG(amount) per customer gives the previous order amount. Subtraction reveals how the customer's spend changed.
query: SELECT customer_id, order_date, amount, amount - LAG(amount) OVER ( PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date ) AS diff FROM orders;

question: Customers who purchased both Product A and Product B
explanation: Filter to only products A and B. COUNT(DISTINCT) = 2 means the customer bought both.
query: SELECT customer_id FROM sales WHERE product_id IN ('A', 'B') GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) = 2;

question: Top N customers by total sales
explanation: Aggregate per customer, sort descending, LIMIT N returns the top-N spenders.
query: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY total_sales DESC LIMIT 5;  -- change to N

question: Month with the highest sales in the current year
explanation: Filter to current year. Aggregate by month. ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1 finds the peak sales month.
query: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', sale_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM sale_date) = EXTRACT(YEAR FROM CURRENT_DATE) GROUP BY month ORDER BY total_sales DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Employees who worked on a project longer than 6 months
explanation: PostgreSQL date arithmetic directly checks if the project duration exceeds 6 months.
query: SELECT employee_id FROM project_assignments WHERE end_date - start_date > INTERVAL '6 months';

question: Find the 5th highest salary
explanation: OFFSET 4 skips the top 4 values. LIMIT 1 picks the 5th. Adjust OFFSET to N-1 for any desired rank.
query: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 4;   -- OFFSET = N-1 for the Nth highest

question: Average salary of employees hired each year
explanation: GROUP BY hire year shows how the average pay of new hires has changed year over year.
query: SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM hire_date) AS year, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY year ORDER BY year;

question: Employees with salaries in the 25th–75th percentile (IQR)
explanation: PERCENTILE_CONT computes exact quartile boundaries. BETWEEN filters the interquartile range (IQR).
query: WITH percentiles AS ( SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS p25, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS p75 FROM employees ) SELECT e.* FROM employees e, percentiles p WHERE e.salary BETWEEN p.p25 AND p.p75;

question: Employees with salary above their department average
explanation: Join employees to a subquery of department averages. Filter where employee salary exceeds the dept average.
query: SELECT e.* FROM employees e JOIN ( SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department_id ) d ON e.department_id = d.department_id WHERE e.salary > d.avg_salary;

question: Difference between each sale and the previous sale (global)
explanation: Global LAG (no partition) computes the change from the previous date's sales row in chronological order.
query: SELECT sale_date, amount, amount - LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date) AS diff FROM sales;

question: Employees with more than 10 years of tenure
explanation: Date arithmetic on hire_date returns an interval. Comparing to 10 years filters long-tenured employees.
query: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE CURRENT_DATE - hire_date > INTERVAL '10 years';

question: Department with the most promotions
explanation: JOIN promotions to employees to get the department. COUNT and LIMIT 1 find the most promotion-active dept.
query: SELECT e.department_id, COUNT(*) AS promotion_count FROM promotions p JOIN employees e ON p.employee_id = e.id GROUP BY e.department_id ORDER BY promotion_count DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Customers who ordered from at least 3 different categories
explanation: JOIN retrieves categories. HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT category_id) >= 3 identifies buyers with diversified purchases.
query: SELECT customer_id FROM sales s JOIN products p ON s.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.category_id) >= 3;

question: Average gap in days between orders per customer
explanation: LAG gives the prior order date per customer. The date difference is the gap. AVG per customer reveals purchase frequency.
query: WITH ordered AS ( SELECT customer_id, order_date, LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS prev FROM orders ), gaps AS (SELECT customer_id, order_date - prev AS gap FROM ordered WHERE prev IS NOT NULL) SELECT customer_id, AVG(gap) AS avg_gap_days FROM gaps GROUP BY customer_id;

question: Customers who never ordered product X
explanation: Subquery finds all customers who bought product X. NOT IN returns everyone who never appears in that list.
query: SELECT customer_id FROM customers WHERE customer_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM sales WHERE product_id = 'X');

question: Total revenue and order count per country
explanation: JOIN customers to orders. GROUP BY country gives geographic breakdown of both order volume and revenue.
query: SELECT c.country, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count, SUM(o.amount) AS total_revenue FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY c.country;

question: Employees hired on the same day as their manager
explanation: Self-join then filter on exact hire_date equality — a rare edge case but a valid interview question.
query: SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager, e.hire_date FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id WHERE e.hire_date = m.hire_date;

question: Top 3 products by quantity sold in each category
explanation: ROW_NUMBER per category ranks products by quantity. rn <= 3 keeps the top 3 per category.
query: SELECT category_id, product_id, total_quantity FROM ( SELECT p.category_id, s.product_id, SUM(s.quantity) AS total_quantity, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY p.category_id ORDER BY SUM(s.quantity) DESC) AS rn FROM sales s JOIN products p ON s.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY p.category_id, s.product_id ) sub WHERE rn <= 3;

question: Days between first and last order per customer
explanation: MAX minus MIN on the date column gives the full span of each customer's ordering history.
query: SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) - MIN(order_date) AS days_between FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id;

question: Customers who increased order frequency for 3 consecutive months
explanation: Two LAG lookbacks confirm three months of strictly increasing order counts — a strong engagement growth signal.
query: WITH monthly_orders AS ( SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '3 months' GROUP BY customer_id, month ), orders_with_lag AS ( SELECT customer_id, month, cnt, LAG(cnt,1) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY month) AS prev1, LAG(cnt,2) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY month) AS prev2 FROM monthly_orders ) SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders_with_lag WHERE cnt > prev1 AND prev1 > prev2;

question: Employees whose salary equals their job title average
explanation: Join to job-title averages. Equality on salary finds the rare cases where someone earns exactly the average.
query: SELECT e.* FROM employees e JOIN ( SELECT job_title, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY job_title ) j ON e.job_title = j.job_title AND e.salary = j.avg_salary;

question: Salary difference between employees and their managers
explanation: Self-join then subtract. Positive = employee earns more; negative = employee earns less than the manager.
query: SELECT e.name, m.name AS manager, e.salary, m.salary AS mgr_salary, e.salary - m.salary AS salary_diff FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

question: Departments with no employees
explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps all departments. e.id IS NULL means no employee is assigned to that department.
query: SELECT d.* FROM departments d LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id WHERE e.id IS NULL;

question: Employee with the max salary in each department
explanation: CTE finds max salary per department. Join back on both dept and exact salary returns all tied top earners.
query: WITH dept_max AS ( SELECT department_id, MAX(salary) AS max_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department_id ) SELECT e.* FROM employees e JOIN dept_max d ON e.department_id = d.department_id AND e.salary = d.max_salary;

question: Customers who ordered every day in the last week
explanation: generate_series creates all 7 days of the past week. Only customers with orders on all 7 days are returned.
query: WITH days AS ( SELECT generate_series( CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '6 days', CURRENT_DATE, INTERVAL '1 day' ) AS day ) SELECT customer_id FROM orders o JOIN days d ON o.order_date = d.day GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_date) = 7;

question: Product with the highest quantity in a single order
explanation: MAX per product finds the peak single-order quantity. ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1 returns the overall leader.
query: SELECT product_id, MAX(quantity) AS max_qty_in_order FROM sales GROUP BY product_id ORDER BY max_qty_in_order DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Employees hired before their department was created
explanation: If hire_date < dept creation_date, the employee joined the company before the department even existed.
query: SELECT e.* FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id WHERE e.hire_date < d.creation_date;

question: Customers with sales in at least 3 different years
explanation: EXTRACT YEAR from each sale; COUNT(DISTINCT) >= 3 identifies long-term loyal customers.
query: SELECT customer_id FROM sales GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM sale_date)) >= 3;

question: Employees earning above company avg but below their dept avg
explanation: Employee earns more than the global average but less than their specific department average.
query: WITH company_avg AS (SELECT AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees), dept_avg AS (SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM employees GROUP BY department_id) SELECT e.* FROM employees e, company_avg ca JOIN dept_avg da ON e.department_id = da.department_id WHERE e.salary > ca.avg_salary AND e.salary < da.avg_salary;

question: Average order amount per customer per year
explanation: Two-dimensional GROUP BY: customer × year. AVG reveals each customer's spending pattern over time.
query: SELECT customer_id, EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) AS year, AVG(amount) AS avg_order_amount FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id, year;

question: Employees who worked on projects with budget over $1 million
explanation: JOIN brings in the project budget. DISTINCT avoids repeating employees assigned to multiple such projects.
query: SELECT DISTINCT pa.employee_id FROM project_assignments pa JOIN projects p ON pa.project_id = p.project_id WHERE p.budget > 1000000;

question: Most recent promotion date per employee
explanation: Simple MAX aggregate over promotion_date returns the most recent promotion per employee.
query: SELECT employee_id, MAX(promotion_date) AS last_promotion_date FROM promotions GROUP BY employee_id;

question: Customers whose total orders exceed the average order amount
explanation: CTE finds the average single-order amount. HAVING compares each customer's total spend against that benchmark.
query: WITH avg_order AS (SELECT AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM orders) SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_amount FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(amount) > (SELECT avg_amount FROM avg_order);

question: Products that have never been ordered
explanation: NOT IN with a subquery cleanly identifies products that are absent from all sales records.
query: SELECT product_id FROM products WHERE product_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT product_id FROM sales);

question: Month with the lowest sales in the past year
explanation: Filter to the last year. Aggregate by month. ORDER BY ascending LIMIT 1 finds the slowest month.
query: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', sale_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales WHERE sale_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year' GROUP BY month ORDER BY total_sales LIMIT 1;

question: Employees hired each month in the last year
explanation: DATE_TRUNC normalises to month boundaries. COUNT per month shows the hiring cadence for the past year.
query: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', hire_date) AS month, COUNT(*) AS hires FROM employees WHERE hire_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year' GROUP BY month ORDER BY month;

question: Department with the highest number of projects
explanation: COUNT per department, ORDER BY descending, LIMIT 1 returns the single busiest department.
query: SELECT department_id, COUNT(*) AS project_count FROM projects GROUP BY department_id ORDER BY project_count DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Employees without any dependents
explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps all employees. A NULL dependent_id means that employee has no registered dependents.
query: SELECT e.* FROM employees e LEFT JOIN dependents d ON e.id = d.employee_id WHERE d.dependent_id IS NULL;

question: Total sales per category including zero-sales categories
explanation: Chained LEFT JOINs keep all categories. COALESCE replaces a NULL SUM with 0 for clean output.
query: SELECT c.category_id, COALESCE(SUM(s.amount), 0) AS total_sales FROM categories c LEFT JOIN products p ON c.category_id = p.category_id LEFT JOIN sales s ON p.product_id = s.product_id GROUP BY c.category_id;

question: Employees who were promoted but salary did not increase
explanation: Correlated subquery fetches the pre-promotion salary. Current salary <= pre-promotion = no raise was given.
query: SELECT e.id, e.name FROM employees e JOIN promotions p ON e.id = p.employee_id WHERE e.salary <= ( SELECT salary_before FROM promotion_history WHERE employee_id = e.id ORDER BY promotion_date DESC LIMIT 1 );

question: Customers with average order amount above $500
explanation: AVG per customer. HAVING > 500 isolates customers with consistently high average order values.
query: SELECT customer_id, AVG(amount) AS avg_order_amount FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING AVG(amount) > 500;

question: Orders where total quantity exceeds 100 units
explanation: Summing item quantities per order. HAVING > 100 identifies bulk purchase orders.
query: SELECT order_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_quantity FROM order_items GROUP BY order_id HAVING SUM(quantity) > 100;

question: Products whose sales doubled vs the previous month
explanation: LAG provides last month's sales for the same product. >= 2x means at least 100 % month-over-month growth.
query: WITH monthly_sales AS ( SELECT product_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', sale_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales GROUP BY product_id, month ), sales_comparison AS ( SELECT product_id, month, total_sales, LAG(total_sales) OVER (PARTITION BY product_id ORDER BY month) AS prev_sales FROM monthly_sales ) SELECT product_id, month FROM sales_comparison WHERE prev_sales IS NOT NULL AND total_sales >= 2 * prev_sales;

question: Employees with more than 3 projects in 2023
explanation: Date filter restricts to 2023. COUNT(DISTINCT) prevents double-counting the same project.
query: SELECT employee_id, COUNT(DISTINCT project_id) AS project_count FROM project_assignments WHERE assignment_date BETWEEN '2023-01-01' AND '2023-12-31' GROUP BY employee_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT project_id) > 3;

question: Customers whose last order was more than 1 year ago
explanation: HAVING on MAX(order_date) filters lapsed customers who have not ordered in over a year.
query: SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) AS last_order_date FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING MAX(order_date) < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year';

question: Average salary increase percentage per department
explanation: Percentage increase = (new − old) / old × 100. AVG per department shows the typical raise size.
query: SELECT e.department_id, AVG((e.salary - p.old_salary) / p.old_salary * 100) AS avg_increase_pct FROM employees e JOIN promotions p ON e.id = p.employee_id GROUP BY e.department_id;

question: Employees who have never been promoted
explanation: Subquery collects all promoted employee IDs. NOT IN returns everyone who never appears in that list.
query: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT employee_id FROM promotions);

question: Products ordered by every customer
explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) = total customers means every single customer bought this product.
query: SELECT product_id FROM sales GROUP BY product_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) = (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM customers);

question: Customers with orders over $5,000 in the last 6 months
explanation: Filter to recent 6 months. SUM per customer. HAVING > 5000 identifies high-value recent customers.
query: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_amount FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '6 months' GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(amount) > 5000;

question: Rank employees by salary within their department
explanation: RANK() per department. Tied salaries share a rank and subsequent positions are skipped (1, 1, 3).
query: SELECT *, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank FROM employees;

question: Customers who purchased a product but never reordered it
explanation: Count purchases per customer-product pair. count = 1 means exactly one purchase — no repeat order.
query: WITH order_counts AS ( SELECT customer_id, product_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM sales GROUP BY customer_id, product_id ) SELECT customer_id, product_id FROM order_counts WHERE order_count = 1;

question: Day with the highest number of new hires
explanation: GROUP BY hire_date counts daily new hires. ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1 finds the peak onboarding day.
query: SELECT hire_date, COUNT(*) AS hires FROM employees GROUP BY hire_date ORDER BY hires DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Employees who worked in more than one department
explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT department_id) > 1 identifies employees who moved between departments over time.
query: SELECT employee_id FROM employee_department_history GROUP BY employee_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT department_id) > 1;

question: Customer who ordered the most products in 2023
explanation: Filter to 2023. SUM quantity per customer. ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1 returns the highest volume buyer.
query: SELECT customer_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_quantity FROM sales WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM sale_date) = 2023 GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY total_quantity DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Average days to ship per shipping method
explanation: Date subtraction gives duration per order. AVG per shipping method reveals fulfillment efficiency.
query: SELECT shipping_method, AVG(shipping_date - order_date) AS avg_shipping_days FROM orders GROUP BY shipping_method;

question: Employees with overlapping project assignments
explanation: Self-join on the same employee. The classic overlap condition detects employees assigned to two simultaneous projects.
query: SELECT pa1.employee_id, pa1.project_id AS project1, pa2.project_id AS project2 FROM project_assignments pa1 JOIN project_assignments pa2 ON pa1.employee_id = pa2.employee_id AND pa1.project_id <> pa2.project_id WHERE pa1.start_date <= pa2.end_date AND pa1.end_date >= pa2.start_date;

question: Total unique customers per product category
explanation: JOIN retrieves category from products. COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) avoids counting repeat buyers.
query: SELECT p.category_id, COUNT(DISTINCT s.customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM sales s JOIN products p ON s.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY p.category_id;

question: Customers whose orders increased 20 %+ month-over-month
explanation: LAG gives the prior month count. >= 1.2x = at least 20 % increase from one month to the next.
query: WITH monthly_orders AS ( SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id, month ), orders_comparison AS ( SELECT customer_id, month, cnt, LAG(cnt) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY month) AS prev FROM monthly_orders ) SELECT customer_id, month FROM orders_comparison WHERE prev IS NOT NULL AND cnt >= 1.2 * prev;

question: Employees with no projects in the last 6 months
explanation: Date condition in the JOIN keeps only recent assignments. NULL project_id = no recent project work.
query: SELECT e.* FROM employees e LEFT JOIN project_assignments pa ON e.id = pa.employee_id AND pa.start_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '6 months' WHERE pa.project_id IS NULL;

question: Employees who changed departments more than twice
explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT department_id) > 2 means at least 3 different departments — an internal job-hopper.
query: SELECT employee_id FROM employee_department_history GROUP BY employee_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT department_id) > 2;

question: Product with the highest average customer rating
explanation: AVG rating per product. ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1 returns the top-rated product.
query: SELECT product_id, AVG(rating) AS avg_rating FROM product_reviews GROUP BY product_id ORDER BY avg_rating DESC LIMIT 1;

question: Customers who ordered but never used a discount
explanation: DISTINCT avoids repeating the same customer. FALSE discount_used identifies full-price buyers.
query: SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders WHERE discount_used = FALSE;
